coredata.dc:title: A carbon dot based metal-free photoelectrochemical cell using O2/H2O redox couple in real seawater
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhaomin
Wu, Zhenyu
Xu, Jia
Zhao, Rui
Han, Xiaoxia
Jiang, Jiwei
Shao, Mingwang
Dong, Bin
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Photochemical devices show great potential in applications ranging from electrical energy storage to future smart grids. Here, we report a metal-free composite film cell based on carbon dots (CDots) and polyaniline (PANI), which could achieve 24/7 discharge and photo-charging in seawater. CDots/PANI film cell has an open-circuit-voltage (OCV) of 0.95 V, a maximum power density of 380 mW m2, and the ability to discharge continuously for 40 h under solar irradiation of AM 1.5 G. It couples the reduction reaction of PANI, and the CDot-catalyzed oxygen reduction reactions (ORR) during discharge. While the light-induced CDot-catalyzed oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and the reduction reaction of PANI during charging. In particular, in-situ TPV tests were used to verify the effect of the light-induced OER and the kinetic properties of the catalyst on the charge transfer at the interface. We believe this research may provide new ideas for sustainable energy conversion and storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
O2/H2O redox couples
Electron/proton transfer
Photo-charging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDots and CDots/PANI composites
The performances of the CDots/PANI composites film cell
Mechanism of the CDots/PANI composites film cell
Application of the CDots/PANI composites film cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM, (b) magnified SEM, (c) TEM images of the CDots/PANI composites film (inset: HRTEM image showing the lattice spacing of CDot). (d) XPS full spectrum of the composites. The high-resolution XPS spectra of CDots/PANI composites: (e) C 1s; (f) N 1s and (g) O 1s peaks. CV of (h) CDots and (i) PANI in N2-saturated TBPA acetonitrile solution with ferrocene as the standard. (j) The band structure of CDots and PANI as compared to the potential of H2/H2O, O2/H2O and the current collector (CP).

Schematic indicating (a) the discharge and (b) the photo-charging of the film cell. (c) The current (black curve) and the OCV (green curve) of the film cell. (d) The discharge of the film cell in the constant current mode (10-6 A) for 40 h. (e) Short circuit current density (black curve) and OCV (green curve) of the film cell are used as the external load resistance. (f) Output power density as a function of external load resistance. (g) Photo-voltage and (h) photo-current curves of CDots/PANI composites film (red curve) and PANI film (black curve). (i) Energy storage curves of the film cell for five cycles. (j) The current (black curve), the OCV (blue curve) and (k) the discharge of the film cell in seawater.

(a) The scheme for the mechanism of the ORR of CDots and the oxidation reaction of PANI coupled to the discharge of the photo-electrochemical film cell. (b) Cyclic voltammograms (CV) curve of PANI. (c) ORR polarization curves of CDots in N2-/O2-saturated 0.1 mol L-1 KOH aqueous solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s-1. (d) RDE linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) curves of CDots at various rotational speeds. (e) The OCV is found to be 0 when a PE film is sandwiched between CDots/PANI film and current collector. (f) The relationship of pH and OCV at the contact interface between the film cell and CP. (g) The OCV of the film cell change with the different contact area between the CP and the film cell. (h) The scheme for the mechanism of the OER of CDots and the reduction reaction of PANI coupled to the photo-charging of the photo-electrochemical film cell. (i) Photo-assisted OER performance of PANI and CDots/PANI composites with or without light irradiation. (j) Tafel plots of the PANI and the CDots/PANI composites with or without light irradiation. (k) TPV relaxation curves and attenuation constants (τ) of charge recombination process of PANI and the CDots/PANI composites. (l) Maximum charge extraction time (tmax) and the maximum electron extraction (A) of the PANI and the CDots/PANI composites. (m,n) Number of photo-generated electrons consumed in the catalytic reaction (ΔQ) under different atmospheres.

(a) Schematic diagram and (c) the photograph showing the film cells discharged in seawater after being connected in series can light up a LED bulb. The number of the film cells connected in series and parallel increases, and so does (b) the OCV and (d) the current.

head.ce:title: A carbon dot based metal-free photoelectrochemical cell using O2/H2O redox couple in real seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photochemical devices show great potential in applications ranging from electrical energy storage to future smart grids. Here, we report a metal-free composite film cell based on carbon dots (CDots) and polyaniline (PANI), which could achieve 24/7 discharge and photo-charging in seawater. CDots/PANI film cell has an open-circuit-voltage (OCV) of 0.95 V, a maximum power density of 380 mW m2, and the ability to discharge continuously for 40 h under solar irradiation of AM 1.5 G. It couples the reduction reaction of PANI, and the CDot-catalyzed oxygen reduction reactions (ORR) during discharge. While the light-induced CDot-catalyzed oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and the reduction reaction of PANI during charging. In particular, in-situ TPV tests were used to verify the effect of the light-induced OER and the kinetic properties of the catalyst on the charge transfer at the interface. We believe this research may provide new ideas for sustainable energy conversion and storage.

Carbon dots-based film cell can achieve 24/7 discharge and photo-charging in seawater, is capable of storing energy and utilizes the redox reaction of polyaniline, the ORR/OER catalyzed by carbon dots for discharge and charging, and TPV tests were used to explore the OER process and catalytic mechanism during charging.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing energy demand, coupled with the need to address environmental pollution [1–3], climate change [4–7], and greenhouse gas emissions [8–11], has led to a growing awareness that our society must emerge to explore the specific alternative, regenerative, and carbon-neutral energy sources to substitute for these fossil fuels [12–17]. Among the different renewable energy sources, solar energy is currently the most representative exploitable green energy source [18–22]; however, given its intermittent nature, it poses many difficult problems for practical applications, and a very attractive strategy is the direct conversion of solar energy [23–29].

For zero CO2 emissions from solar power processes, water and oxygen are undoubtedly the ideal active species [30,31], and are ideal due to their good diffusion properties and photo-chemical stability, and have advantages in terms of cleanliness, eco-friendliness and natural abundance [32–37]. However, solar power generation via photo-chemical cells using exclusively oxygen/water (O2/H2O) redox couple is very challenging due to the uphill and sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and extremely difficult to extract electrons from water molecules [38–42]. Carbon dots (CDots) are a good photo-responsive material with unique photo-electric effect and high catalytic activity for various reactions, which can catalyze the OER reaction under light [43–50]. In addition, CDots-based electrochemical cells can show higher open-circuit voltages owing to more aggressive redox potentials (1.23 V vs. RHE) [51,52].

Herein, we report a CDot-based photo-electrochemical cell for 24/7 discharge and photo-charging in seawater, reaching a voltage of 0.95 V after 4 h of photo-charging and discharges continuously for 40 h. The reversible OER and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) based on CDots and the reversible redox reaction of PANI are used for photo-charging and discharging, where the effect of light-induced OER reaction and the kinetic properties of catalyst charge transfer at the interface are investigated using in-situ transient photovoltage (TPV) experiments. Interestingly, the film cells can be connected in series to light up a LED bulb in seawater and charge and discharge in seawater 24/7, charging during the day and discharging during the night. We believe that this current investigation could open up new possibilities for the design, utilization and development of sustainable energy conversion and storage technologies.

Graphite rods were obtained from Alfa Aesar Company. Aniline, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Mw = 9000–10,000), Glutaraldehyde, ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl36H2O), Nafion, ferrocene, hydrogen chloride (HCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), ammonium persulphate ((NH4)2S2O8), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), potassium hydroxide (KOH), tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAP) and acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from Aladdin Company. The seawater was taken from the Yellow Sea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDots and CDots/PANI composites
ce:section.ce:para: The graphite rod was applied with a bias voltage to prepare a CDots solution [53]. The original solution was stirred continually for 30 days and left to stand for seven days. Then, the precipitate of graphite particles was filtered by low-speed quantitative filter paper, and the clear solution was separated by centrifugation three times (12,000 rpm). In the end, the separated CDots solution was freeze-dried into particles (with the diameter of 5–10 nm) and redispersed in water to prepare a solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg mL-1.

0.8 g of the PVA were dissolved in 15 mL of water, 100 µL of HCl (1 mol L-1) was added with the PVA solution at 60 ℃ and stirred for 1 h. Under an ice water bath, aniline (100 µL) and CDots solution (2 mL, 1 mg mL-1) were dropped to the mixture, respectively. Then, 0.6 g of FeCl30.6 H2O was added to it and kept at 0 ℃ and ultrasonically dispersed for 24 h. After that, glutaraldehyde was added to cross-link the hydrogels. The resulting solution was pureed by centrifugation (13,000 rpm) and re-dispersed in 15 mL of water. And the prepared dispersion solution was spin-coated onto a glass slide (2.5 cm × 7.5 cm) and kept dry in a constant temperature oven at 25 ℃.

The CDots/PANI composites were synthesized by the oxidation polymerization of aniline in the presence of the CDots (Fig. S1). The CDots/PANI composites have the porous structure, showing from the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images in Fig. 1a,b and Fig. S2. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore structure of CDots/PANI and PANI were characterized by N2 adsorption-desorption measurements at 77 K, where the BET surface area of CDots/PANI (Fig. S3a, red line, 33.20 m2 g-1) is higher than that of PANI (Fig. S3a, black line, 18.75 m2 g-1), demonstrating that the addition of CDots significantly increases the specific surface area of the material. In addition, the CDots/PANI composites have an abundant microporous structure shown in Fig. S3b, which coincides with the pore size distribution in Fig. 1b. Note that the formation of the pores may facilitate the adsorption of water molecules for ORR/OER reactions. The CDots/PANI composites consists of homogeneously distributed CDot with a size of 5 nm, as indicated in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 1c). The CDot in the composite has a crystalline structure, as proved by the (100) lattice of graphitic carbon (the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm) displayed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Fig. 1c, inset).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) further confirmed the composition of the composites with characteristic peaks of CDots (3467 cm−1 (O-H), 1622 cm−1 (CO), 1399 cm−1 (-COO)) and the PANI in the reduced state (3631 cm−1 (N-H), 1352 cm−1 (C-N+)) (Fig. S4). The presence of CDots and PANI could also be confirmed through the XPS study, in which the characteristic N 1s (N-H at 400.8 eV which is derived from proton doped PANI) and O 1s (at 531.7 eV, 532.3 eV, 533.4 eV which can be attributed to the C-O of PVA [54], surface oxygen (Osur) and adsorbed oxygen species (Oads) on the surface of CDot, respectively) can be identified, as shown in Fig. 1d–g. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s show four dissociation peaks at 284.7, 285.3, 286.2, and 288.8 eV, which could be attributed to C-C/CC, C-N/CN, C-O and CO, respectively. Moreover, the UV-Vis spectrum of the composites (blue curve) in Fig. S5 display the broad absorption band in the whole spectrum range and the absorption peaks at 211 nm (from the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in CDots), 250 nm (derived from π-π* transition of the benzene in PANI) and 705 nm (n-π* transition of the quinone in PANI) further confirm the presence of CDots and PANI.

The CDot and PANI exhibit the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of − 6.46 eV (HOMO)/− 3.92 eV (LUMO) (CDot) and − 6.23 eV (HOMO)/− 3.83 eV (LUMO) (PANI), respectively, which based on the cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves of the onset oxidation potential (Eox) and the onset reduction potential (Ered) were determined (Fig. 1h,i, and Section 1 shows the more detailed calculation). According to band structure diagram shown in Fig. 1j, the electrons could be moved from the higher LUMO level of PANI to current collector (carbon paper, CP) first and then to the CDots. A Schottky barrier and the potential difference are generated between the CP and the PANI semiconductor according to the different work functions [55,56]. It can also be seen that the LUMO of both CDot and PANI is more positive than the potentials of H+ and O2 reduction and their HOMO is more negative than the potentials of H2O oxidation, implying that the unique carbon material have the ability to undergo oxidize water and reduce oxygen [57,58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performances of the CDots/PANI composites film cell
ce:section.ce:para: The film can function as an electrochemical secondary cell with CDots/PANI composites and CP as the two electrodes and the current collector, respectively as shown in Fig. 2a,b. The cell illustrated an open-circuit voltage (OCV) and current of 0.95 V and 0.1 mA in air, respectively, as shown in the Fig. 2c, which is higher than all the cells reported using O2/H2O redox couples. In particular, the OCV is independent of the oxidant used to synthesize PANI in the film (Fig. S6). Furthermore, the increase in CDot concentration was followed by an increase in OCV of the composites film (Fig. S7), this suggests that the CDot content controls the reaction rate. Not only that, the film cell can be constantly discharged in air for 40 h (∼0.95 V, as shown in the constant-current mode in Fig. 2d), after which the voltage decreases to 0.4 V. Since the voltage after discharging, i.e. 0.4 V, is irrelevant with the content of the CDots in the film cell (Fig. S7), which indicates that it is purely driven by the Schottky contact, i.e. the difference in the work function between the CP and the PANI. As the contact area between the film and the CP increases, the discharge time also grows (Fig. S8), demonstrating that the location of the reaction is at the contact interface between the CP and the film. The film cell could power the resistance with an OCV and a maximum power density of 0.96 V and 380 mW/m2 in Fig. 2e,f, respectively. The energy density of the film cell are calculated to be 6.92 Wh kg-1, as shown in Section S2 and Fig. S9, which are among the best values in the literatures (Tables S1 and S2).

The film cell could be photo-charging (Fig. 2b). When illuminating the CDots/PANI composites film cell with light after its discharging, both the current and the OCV will increase, as shown in Fig. 2g, h. The fully discharged cell could be fully photo-charged after 4 h light exposure, as demonstrated by the increase in OCV to 0.95 V (Fig. S10). Moreover, the film cell could be repetitively discharged and re-charged (Fig. 2i) and there are negligible changes in the discharging current after 5 times, indicating its excellent cycling stability. In addition, the CDots/PANI composites before discharge, after discharge and after photo-charging were examined by utilizing SEM image and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. As can be seen from Fig. S11, there is negligible change in both the porous morphology of the PANI and the crystal structure of the CDots, indicating the superior stability of the catalyst after the photoelectrochemical performance. More interestingly, the film cell can still function in seawater. And the electrochemical film cell in seawater could achieve the OCV and current of 0.64 V and 0.03 mA (Fig. 4j), respectively. And the cell can be continuously discharged in seawater for 40 h (∼0.63 V, constant current mode), as can be seen from the V-T curve shown in Fig. 2k. The fully discharged film cell in seawater could also be photo-charging. After the light radiation for 4 h, the OCV of the photo-charged cell could be recovered to 0.63 V in seawater (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the CDots/PANI composites film cell
ce:section.ce:para: When discharged, the CDots/PANI composites film cell is likely operated based on the oxidation reaction of PANI and the ORR reaction of CDots ( Fig. 3a). Among others, PANI could undergo reversible transitions between different doping states, as can be seen from the two reversible characteristic reduction and oxidation waves (A/A’ at around 0.2 and B/B’ at 0.6 V, respectively) shown in the CV curve (Fig. 3b) [59]. As shown in the Fig. S13, the CV curves for the weakened reduction peak (∼0.2 V) and the enhanced oxidation peak (∼0.6 V) of PANI before and after discharge shows the green protonated emeraldine PANI (EM) loses electrons and protons to form the violet pernigraniline base (PB, Fig. S14). The reduction of the PB to the EM after the cell discharging is also confirmed by the red-shifts of the UV–vis absorption peak (Fig. S15). It is worth noting that this process produces protons, which originate from the protonated PANI.

CDots could catalyze the ORR reaction, as evidenced by the fact that a single cathodic reduction peak of CDots in the CV curve in O2-saturated KOH solutions and the capacitive behavior can only be seen in N2-saturated solutions (Fig. 3c). The electron transfer number (n) of CDots is estimated to be 2 according to current measurements by the RDE (Fig. 3d, more detailed calculation process is shown in Section S3 and Fig. S16), showing the one step 2e- ORR process to produce H2O2. The ORR reaction occurs on CDots and consumes protons that originates from the EM, as evidenced by the increase in pH during the discharging process (Fig. S17). In addition, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) under non-Faraday conditions was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) to estimate the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA). According to the CV of the sample at different scan rates (Fig. S18) and the equation for the ECSA = Cdl/Cs, Cdl and ECSA of CDots/PANI composites were calculated to be 0.471 mF cm-2 and 21.409 cm2 in 6 M KOH (see Section S4 for more detailed calculations), respectively, which indicate that CDots/PANI has a significantly active surface area and active sites over other catalysts. Note that all reactions (oxidation of PANI and ORR on CDots) occur on the contact interface form the film as well as the CP, as evidenced by the fact that the voltage is not generated by adding a polyethylene (PE) film between the film and the collector (Fig. 3e). Since the proton is consumed at the area covered by the current collector and remains intact at the uncovered area, thus creating a proton concentration gradient across the film where protons migrate, leading to voltage formation. A model was developed to demonstrate the proton diffusion in the film cell (Fig. S19). And proton gradient may lead to the generation of the voltage (see Section S5 for more seed calculations) according to the differential equation of Fick's second law, which could be obtained by the equation:
(1)
U = 4.82 ×
10
4
yz
C
0
D
t
R
Where D is the diffusion coefficient, t is the diffusion time, y is the width and z is the thickness of the film, respectively, C
0 is the original concentration of proton and R is the resistance of the film. It could be seen that in Eq. (1), the voltage is proportional to the initial proton concentration and increases with the increasing proton concentration. The OCV of CDots/PANI composites film depends on its acidity. The OCV increases with the increasing acidity (realized by adding solutions with different pH values at the contact interface), which is likely because an increase in acidity leading to an increase in the proton gradient, as well as an increase in voltage (Fig. 3f). The OCV of the film cell also depends on the distance from the cathodes (i.e., the proton diffusion distance), and the OCV could reach 0.97 V when the pH is 1. As shown in Fig. 3g and Fig. S20, the OCV increases as the distance between the cathodes increases, which proves the proton migration (distance dependent on distance) produces a voltage, which is consistent with the results calculated in Eq. (1). And The OCV could reach 1.01 V when the distance is 7.5 cm.

On the other hand, when charged, the CDots/PANI composites film cell may operate based on the OER reaction of CDots and reduction reaction of PANI (Fig. 3h). To demonstrate that CDots can indeed promote electron-hole separation, a series of photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed. The fluorescence intensity of CDots/PANI is significantly lower than that of PANI, which indicates that increased charge separation and transfer occur in CDots/PANI and CDots can effectively reduce the radiative recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Fig. S21a). In addition, the lifetimes of the electron-hole pairs were investigated by time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurements. As shown in Fig. S21b, the corresponding PL lifetimes (obtained by fitting the spectra with a bi-exponential decay curve) are 1.03 and 2.56 ns for the PANI and CDots/PANI composites, respectively. The longer PL lifetime in the case of CDots/PANI implies that CDots can improve the charge separation efficiency. And the high-energy photons are absorbed by the CDots/PANI composites film, which has excellent photo-response property could absorb the light radiation, generating photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The hole oxidizes the H2O molecule on the CDots to produce oxygen (CDot catalyzed OER reaction), protons and electron, as shown in the LSV curve (Fig. S22). Furthermore, Fig. 3i demonstrates that the CDots/PANI composites have the best photo-responsive properties with the better OER performance than PANI, as illustrated by the higher photocurrent density. Also, CDots accelerated the reaction kinetics and significantly enhanced the photo-assisted ability of CDots/PANI composites in OER due to the much lower Tafel slope (Fig. 3j) of the composites in visible light (198 mV dec-1) compared to the slope in dark conditions (290 mV dec-1) after the addition of CDots. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was utilized to further explore the interfacial charge transfer between CDots/PANI composites (red curve) and PANI (black curve). As shown in Fig. S23, the addition of CDots reduces the charge transfer resistance, as evidenced by the smaller radius of the circular arc of the CDots/PANI composites as compared to that of PANI, suggesting that CDots can accelerate charge separation and transfer and thereby improving the photocatalytic performance. In-situ TPV reveals the kinetic properties of charge transfer at the catalyst interface. Fig. 3k shows the in-situ TPV relaxation curves of CDots, PANI and CDots/PANI composites, respectively, which shows that the addition of CDots promotes the separation of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs, the overall electron-hole recombination rate of CDots/PANI composites is slower than that of PANI, and the time decay constant of CDots/PANI composites (τ2, 0.369 ms) is significantly larger than that of CDots (τ3, 0.274 ms) and PANI (τ1, 0.205 ms) confirming this. Moreover, when irradiated under visible light, the CDots/PANI composites can generate more electrons to participate in the reaction, which is illustrated by the longer maximum charge extraction time (tmax2) of CDots/PANI composites than that of PANI (tmax1) in Fig. 3l. More importantly, the faster electron transfer rate and slower charge recombination rate of the CDots/PANI composites also facilitates the catalytic reaction, which is confirmed by the much higher electron extraction of the composites (A2, 0.0238) than that of PANI (A1, 0.0063).

Fig. 3m,n shows the in-situ TPV patterns of the CDots/PANI composites under different reaction conditions. The amount of photo-generated electrons of the composites in the O2 atmosphere is significantly smaller than that in the N2 atmosphere (ΔQ1 = 0.177), indicating that electrons are consumed in O2 and an ORR reaction occurs, while the amount of photo-generated electrons in the water atmosphere is larger than that in the N2 atmosphere (ΔQ2 = 0.342), proving that an OER reaction takes place and water is oxidized to produce electrons. In addition, electrons and protons are transferred to PB, which undergoes a reduction reaction to EM, as confirmed by the decrease of the oxidation peak and the enhancement of the reduction peak in the CV curves before and after photo-charging (Fig. S24) and the blue-shift of the UV-Vis absorption peak (Fig. S25).

On the basis of the above investigations, we give the following provisional charge/discharge mechanism. During the discharge, PANI undergoes oxidation reaction, releasing protons and electrons. Due to the difference in work function of CP and CDot, electrons are transported from CP to the CDots, where combine protons for the ORR catalytic reaction. Protons are gradually consumed and a proton concentration gradient is formed on the PANI film, creating a voltage, and the reaction stops only when the protons are exhausted. During charging, the CDots undergoes OER reaction due to photogenerated electrons, generating protons and electrons to reduce the oxidized state of PANI to the initial reduced state. In addition, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were applied to elucidate the mechanism, by which the CDots leads to higher ORR/OER activity shown in the Section 6 and Fig. S26.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CDots/PANI composites film cell
ce:section.ce:para: By connecting the cells in series, the CDots/PANI composites film cells can generate electricity in seawater and photo-charging, as shown in Fig. 4a. With 4 cells connected in series, the OCV can reach 2.6 V (Fig. 4b) and can light up an LED lamp (Fig. 4c). What’s more, the composites film cells could also be connected in parallel and the current can reach 0.4 mA (Fig. 4d) after connecting 4 cells in parallel. After 40 h of discharge, the OCV of the cell drops to 0.4 V, while after 4 h of light radiation, the OCV returns to the original 0.95 V and can be cycled 20 times (Fig. S27), indicating that the film cell could be repeatedly discharged in seawater.

In summary, we report a metal-free composite film cell that is based CDots and PANI, which achieves 24/7 discharge and photo-charging in seawater. The film cell is able to convert light energy into chemical energy, store the chemical energy and does not produce any pollution. The cell can achieve a VOC of 0.95 V and a power density of 380 mW m2 and could be continuously discharged for 40 h. In addition, the cell can be fully charged after 4 h of light irradiation. The charging and discharging are based on the reversible OER and ORR of CDots and the reversible redox reaction of PANI. Importantly, the effect of the light-induced OER reaction and the kinetic properties of the catalyst on charge transfer at the interface were investigated using in-situ TPV tests. Interestingly, the cells in series could light up an LED lamp in seawater. This ongoing research provides new inspiration and understanding for the design and synthesis of efficient carbon materials, while opening a new pathway for the design, development and utilization of efficient and sustainable energy conversion and storage technologies.

Zhaomin Chen: Experiments, Measurement, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Zhenyu Wu: Experiments, Measurement, Writing – original draft. Jia Xu: Investigation, Data curation. Rui Zhao: Software, Formal analysis. Xiaoxia Han: Software, Formal analysis. Jiwei Jiang: Software, Formal analysis. Mingwang Shao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Bin Dong: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21574094, 51725204, 21771132, 51972216 and 52041202), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Key R & D program of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (2022BEG02006), Ningxia Autonomous Region Flexible Introduction of Science and Technology Innovation Team (2021RXTDLX08), the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials. It was also supported by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), the 111 Project, Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices, the Fund for Excellent Creative Research Teams of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.apcatb.2022.121914.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
